State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 307 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660          322-5901          322-4441          322-5444

May 26, 1983


Howard, Finn
Los Angeles City Councilmember
City Hall, Room 290
Los Angeles, CA 90012

                                        Re: Advice No. 83-136

Dear Mr. Finn:

     You have requested advice from the Commission pursuant to
Government Code Section 83114(b) as to whether you may vote on a
motion pending before the City Council to downzone certain
property in Shadow Hills.

FACTS

     The facts which have been presented to me are as follows:
You own a number of parcels of property in Shadow Hills,
including a parcel located at 9455 Hillrose Street. James B.
Moore owns a parcel at 9505 La Canada Way in Shadow Hills. Your
property is approximately 700 feet away from the Moore
property. It is not adjacent to the Moore property, and the
Moore property is not visible from your property. Your property
is on the other side of a hill from the Moore property, and
faces away from that property. Your property is undeveloped.

     The Moore property is located at the intersection of
La Canada Way and Sunland Boulevard. This property is presently
zoned to permit retail commercial use or multiple residential
use (C2-1-K). Other property in the immediate area on Sunland
Boulevard is also zoned C2-1-K, and has some commercial use.
The surrounding property, including yours, is zoned for
horsekeeping and low density residential use (RA-1-K). The
owner of the Moore property plans to use it for construction of
a 47-unit apartment complex. At least some of the planned
apartments would be for low and moderate income residents.

     As a member of the City Council, you introduced a motion to
downzone the Moore property from retail commercial and multiple
residential use (C2-1-K) to low density residential use
(RA-1-K). The effect of the proposed downzoning would be to
prevent the construction of the 47-unit apartment complex on the
Moore property.

Howard Finn
May 26, 1983
Page 2


At the request of the City Attorney, two Los Angeles City
Assessors, Joseph A. Gary and Richard W. Brown, assessed the
potential impact of the downzoning on your properties. In their
reports, both Mr. Gary and Mr. Brown described in detail their
factual observations and the basis for their opinions. We have
reviewed both reports.

The first report was made by Joseph Gary. He based his
report on his observations of the properties in question, on
Planning Department recommendations and on discussions with
local real estate brokers. He stated that the downzoning of the
Moore property would beneficially affect the contiguous
properties. Properties which are not adjacent to the Moore
property, or from which the Moore property is not visible, would
not be affected by the downzoning. Thus, Mr. Gary concluded
that the downzoning would have no effect on the value of your
property in Shadow Hills, including your property on Hillrose
Street.

Mr. Gary stated that his evaluation of the effect of the
construction of the planned apartment complex was speculative
and conjectural. He stated that the proposed project would
affect the value of surrounding properties, although possibly
only on a temporary basis. The effect could be as far as
your Hillrose Street property (but not to your other Shadow
Hills property). However, he stated that your property, being
low in value for the area, not suitable for horsekeeping, and
out of sight of the project, would be affected less than other
property in the area.

Mr. Brown based his report on observation of the properties,
discussion with four realtors within a two mile area of the
Moore property, and on the experience of the local realtors with
other housing projects and condominiums which had recently been
built in the area. These other projects had had very little
effect on surrounding property values. What effect they did
have was limited to the adjacent properties. It was Mr. Brown's
conclusion that neither the downzoning nor the construction of
the proposed apartment complex would affect the value of your
Hillrose Street property or any other property you own in Shadow
Hills. He stated that the effects, if any, of the downzoning or
of the project would be limited to properties immediately
adjacent to, and visible from, the project.

Subsequent to your request to us for advice in this matter,
litigation has been commenced on the same issue by Mr. Moore,
through his attorney Mr. Nicholas. We have been furnished with
copies of some of the pleadings, including a declaration of

Howard Finn
May 26, 1983
Page 3


Nadine McNulty. The question of the effect of the downzoning
was reviewed by Ms. McNulty, a real estate salesperson in
Los Angeles, in her declaration submitted to the court. She
stated that she reviewed the proposed apartment project and some
30 properties owned by you and/or related individuals or
entities in the Shadow Hills area. Her opinion is that both the
downzoning and the proposed project would have a material
financial effect on your properties, particularly on your
Hillrose Street property (less than 800 feet from the Moore
property) and your Wentworth Street property (2,600 feet from
the Moore property). She gives no estimate of the amount of the
effect, nor does she provide any additional factual information
about these properties, or about the factors upon which she
bases her conclusion.

We have also considered the materials attached to your
letter and the complaint1/ of Mr. Nicholas.

ANALYSIS

Government Code Section 871002/ requires a public official
to disqualify himself from participation in any decision in
which he has a financial interest. An official has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on any real property in
which the official has an investment of more than $1,000. Thus,
you will have to disqualify yourself from the decision on the
proposed downzoning if the following four conditions are met:

(1) you have an interest of over $1,000 in real
property;

(2) it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a financial effect on the real property;

(3) the effect will be material; and

(4) the effect will be different than the effect on
the public generally.

_____________
1/ Both the complaint and your request are being treated
by the Commission in an advice context. There is no active FPPC
enforcement investigation of this matter.

2/ All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

Howard Finn
May 26, 1983
Page 4


     The first condition is clearly met by your ownership of the
Hillrose Street property.

     The second condition, on the basis of the facts available to
us, does not appear to be met. Both assessors have agreed that
the downzoning will have no effect on the value of any of your
property. One assessor states that the proposed apartment
project would have no effect on your property. The other says
that it might have a temporary effect of unknown amount on your
Hillrose Street property; he also says that such an effect is
"speculative and conjectural." In order for an effect to be
"reasonably foreseeable," there must be some substantial
probability that it will occur. Mere conjecture or speculation
is not sufficient. Opinion requested by Tom Thorner, 1 PPPC
Opinions 198, 203-204 (1975).

     It is true that Nadine McNulry concluded that the downzoning
and the project would have a material financial effect on your
properties. However, unlike the assessors, she merely stated
her conclusion; she did not provide any facts upon which such a
conclusion could be based. Therefore, her declaration has not
caused us to question the facts provided by you or the facts in
the assessors' reports. Her opinion on the legal issue of
whether a conflict exists, absent supporting facts, does not
affect our conclusion.

     In addition to the facts presented by the assessors'
reports, we also have your letter, a complaint filed by
Mr. Nicholas, a map of the area and other documents. None of
these items provided any additional information which was
helpful to us in determining how the proposed downzoning or the
apartment project would affect your properties.

     Based upon the facts available to us, it does not appear to
be reasonably foreseeable that either the proposed downzoning,
or the construction of the proposed apartment complex, will have
a financial effect upon the value of your properties.
Therefore, it is not necessary to consider the issues of
materiality or of the public generally. Our advice to you is
that you do not need to disqualify yourself from the decisions
on the proposed downzoning as a result of your real property
investments in Shadow Hills.

     I would like to emphasize that this advice is based on the
facts that you and the City Attorney have provided. This agency
is not a fact finding agency; we do not resolve disputed issues
of fact. Our advice is based on our legal analysis of the facts
as stated; if the facts turn out to be different than those

Howard Finn
May 26, 1983
Page 5


provided, our advice may change. Of course, the court can
resolve factual disputes. If the court makes a different
determination from our interpretation, you should not rely on
our advice. In the meantime, so long as you have requested
advice in good faith, and have disclosed truthfully all material
facts, you are protected by the provisions of Section 83114(b)
if you act in reliance on our advice.

Some question was also raised in Mr. Nicholas' complaint and
in your letter as to the possible effects of the downzoning
decision on your business interests. You have stated that you
have had no business activities in the area since 1980. On the
basis of that statement, and in the absence of any facts showing
that the downzoning would have an effect on your business, we
conclude that you have no potential conflict of interest in the
downzoning decision as a result of your business interests.

I hope this letter has answered your questions. I will be
happy to discuss this matter if you have any further questions.

Very truly yours,


Barbara A. Milman
Chief
Legal Division

BAM:plh
cc: Tony Alperin, City Attorney's Office
    Los Angeles
    William Nicholas, Esq.

OFFICE OF

CITY ATTORNEY
CITY HALL EAST
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90012

May 24 | 38 PM '03

IRA REINER
CITY ATTORNEY


May 23, 1983


EXPRESS MAIL DELIVERY


BARBARA MILMAN, ESQ.
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street
Sacramento, California  95814

     Enclosed please find the following in connection with
the advice you are preparing for Councilman Howard Finn:

     1.  Report from Joseph Gary;

     2.  Report from Richard Brown;

     3.  Declaration of Nadine McMulty;

     4.  Plaintiffs' Points and Authorities in Pair Avenue
Development Co. v. City of Los Angeles.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        IRA REINER, City Attorney

                                        By [signature]
                                           ANTHONY SAUL ALPERIN
                                           Deputy City Attorney

ASA:dpl

Enclosure

FGRM GEN. 160 (Rev. 6-80)

CITY OF LOS ANGELES

INTER-DEPARTMENTAL CORRESPONDENCE

Date:       May 17, 1983

To:         GARY NETZER, Land Use and Environment Division
            City Attorney's Office

From:       JOSEPH A. GARY, Real Estate Officer
            Real Estate Division, Bureau of Engineering

Subject:    Down Zoning of Property at Sunland Boulevard and
            La Canada Way


            There are two issues, as I understand from Anthony Alperin,
Deputy City Attorney, involved in this down zoning action.
The first is the impact of a change in zone from C2-1-K to
RA-1-K of the James B. Moore property on the surrounding area,
and particularly on one of Councilman Howard Finn's lots
located approximately seven hundred feet away (northwesterly).
The second issue would obtain if the zoning of the Moore
property were to remain C2-1-K and a forty-seven unit apart-
ment complex were to be constructed on it.  The question is,
what would be the impact of the proposed development on the
surrounding land and particularly on Councilman Finn's pro-
perty mentioned above.

From an appraisal point of view, the impact of the down zoning
is far simpler to assess than the impact of the proposed pro-
ject.  The second issue is speculative and conjectural and
therefore can be evaluated in only a general way.  For this
investigation I have relied upon my observations of the
Shadow Hills area, Planning Department recommendations and
discussions with local real estate brokers.

Shadow Hills is a canyon with flat, rolling and hilly terrain.
Most of the flat and rolling area is divided into minimum
half acre horsekeeping, residential properties.  The inter-
section at Sunland Boulevard and La Canada Way is one of
perhaps three commercially zoned and developed areas in
Shadow Hills.  In keeping with the rural atmosphere, the
commercial properties are also low density.

The Moore property was developed with a single family residence
until Moore purchased it in April 1980.  The residence was
demolished sometime after purchase and the site is now unimproved.
Between this property and Councilman Finn's lot, the properties
are zoned and developed for residential-horsekeeping.  The
terrain is rolling and the properties, i.e., Councilman Finn's
(9455 Hillrose Street) and the Moore property (9505 La Canada
Way) are not in sight of each other.  Councilman Finn's lot

GARY NETZER
Page 2                                           May 17, 1983


is on a hillside, downsloping from Hillrose Street, with
little natural building pad and no potential for horse-
keeping. The view from the site is across the Foothill
Freeway to the San Gabriel Mountains.

Councilman Finn's lot also fronts on Wentworth Street at
the bottom of the hill from Hillrose Street. While some
of the neighboring properties with the same double frontage
have been developed on the Wentworth Street side, this might
be a viable option to development on the hill. If this is
so, the residence would be outside the horsekeeping neighbor-
hood. I have not investigated the potential for development
along Wentworth Street.

The low density and rural atmosphere of Shadow Hills are the
distinctive characteristics of the area. Consequently, if
physical characteristics were similar, the properties away
from the commercial and high travelled areas are preferable,
and therefore, have a higher value, than those immediately
adjacent. The brokers also agreed to the corollary of this,
i.e., that the down zoning of a site from C2 to RA would
increase the value of those adjacent RA sites. Without sales
of properties in the immediate vicinity, it is impossible to
precisely delineate the affected area; but I believe the down
zoning of the Moore property would beneficially affect the
contiguous properties and those across La Canada Way to
Radwin Avenue. Beyond that and particularly out of sight of
the property, I would expect no change in value. That con-
clusion applies to the Finn property on Hillrose.

There is no development in Shadow Hills similar to the pro-
posed forty-seven unit complex discussed above. The brokers'
consensus of opinion is that the proposed project would
adversely affect surrounding property value. The impact
could easily extend northerly of Radwin Avenue (that is,
beyond one block). One broker believes there would "always
be people in the hills". My observations from my investi-
gation are as follows:

     1. hillside, non-horsekeeping one-half acre sites,
        such as Councilman Finn's, are being listed for
        approximately $30,000.00 and the one-half acre
        horsekeeping sites for $60,000.00 to $70,000.00.

     2. being at the lowest value level in the area and
        atypical of the predominant use in the area,
        the hillside lots would be less affected by the
        proposed development.

GARY NETZER
Page 3

May 17,1985

3.  the distance from the Moore property and the
rolling terrain would also mitigate the impact
of the proposed project.

My conclusion is that though Councilman Finn's lot may suffer
a decline in value from Moore's proposed project, possibly
only temporarily, its loss will be similar to other hillside
lots and that the decline will be less than for the horse-
keeping properties in the area.


JAG:ad

APPROVED:

FC*M GEN 160 (REV. 2-75)

CITY OF LOS ANGELES
INTER-DEPARTMENTAL CORRESPONDENCE

Date: May 20, 1983

To: GARY NETZER, Assistant City Attorney
    City Attorney's Office

From: RICHARD W. BROWN, Real Estate Officer
       Real Estate Division, Bureau of Engineering

Subject: Downzoning of Property at Sunland Boulevard and
         La Canada Way

Per your request an investigation was made as to the possible
effect of the downzoning of the property at the northerly
corner of La Canada Place and La Canada Way upon the values
of neighboring properties and most specifically upon the
property known as Fraction of Lot 11 of Tract 5776 which
is owned by Los Angeles City Councilman Howard Finn. Said
downzoning would change the zoning of the lot at the northerly
corner of La Canada Place and La Canada Way northerly of
Sunland Boulevard from C2-1K to RA-1K. The former zoning
would permit retail commercial uses as well as multiple
residential uses. The less intensive RA-1K zoning is a
suburban residential zone and would, in effect, limit the
lot (Lot 51 Tract 5776) to one residence. The K designation
permits horsekeeping. It is understood that the owner of
Lot 51 Tract 5776 had been planning to develop his property
with a 47 unit low and moderate income housing project.

To answer the question at hand, one must actually approach
the problem from the opposite direction. Would development
as planned have an effect, positive or negative, upon the
value of Mr. Finn's property. The reason for this is rooted
in the concept of homogeneity and its effect upon value.
Generally speaking, unaltered value patterns are observed in
areas of homogeneous development. Should the lot be downzoned
and developed in accordance with its RA-1K zoning, this would
be in keeping with the neighborhood and no change in neighbor-
hood values would take place; thus, the determination must be
looked upon from the effect of a non-homogeneous development,
a 47 unit complex, upon the value of the area. If such a
development would negatively affect the values in the area,
then non-development would by comparison have a positive effect.

To begin this analysis one must understand the nature of the
area in which this takes place -- Shadow Hills. Shadow Hills
is a section of the extreme northeasterly section of the
San Fernando Valley. It extends from Wentworth Street on the
north to Sunland Boulevard on the south and from Foothill
Boulevard on the east to approximately Stonehurst Avenue on
the west. The most unique aspect of this area is its devotion

GARY NETZER                                     May 20, 1983
Page 2


to horses.  Far and away the vast majority of the residences
are zoned for and are in fact keeping horses.  Many of the
streets are dirt which is in keeping with the wishes of the
residents who want to protect their horses from shin splints.
Through the middle of this area running east and west is a
range of small hills.  These have the effect of orienting
properties either toward Sunland Boulevard or toward Wentworth
Street and the Tujunga Wash.  The property to be downzoned is
oriented toward the former; Mr. Finn's is oriented toward the
latter.

The appraiser went to the local real estate community to get
opinions as to the possible effect of a 47 unit project on the
value of surrounding horse oriented properties.  The appraiser
talked to Mary Ann Michelsen of Watkins, Winslow Associates;
June Backstrom, Ray Cormier, and Ray Carter, of Red Carpet
Realtors; and Allen Blair of Century 21 Real Estate.  All are
located within 2 miles of the properties in question.

Ms. Michelsen indicated that the major concern of the people
in the Shadow Hills area was increased traffic congestion and
the accompanying threat to the horses in the area.  She felt,
however, that if any damage were to occur it would be to those
properties either abutting the development or those along
La Canada Way.  The first suffering a loss of privacy, the second
enduring the increase in vehicular traffic.  She felt that Mr.
Finn's property was sufficiently far away (700+ feet) and
sufficiently oriented toward Wentworth Street and the Tujunga
Wash as to be unaffected by such a development.  Additionally,
Mr. Finn's lot is a steep downhill lot ot the north and a
potential buyer would not probably desirous of developing it
for horsekeeping which appears to be the main concern of most
others in the area.

Ms. Backstrom and Messrs. Carter and Cormier agreed with this
position noting that the huge condominium and apartment complex
built on Sunland Boulevard just easterly of the Foothill Freeway
have had no appreciable effect upon the values of the single family
residential streets in the vicinity with the exception of a few
houses on Sherman Grove Avenue, Newhome Avenue and Wyngate Street.
In each case, the houses which were negatively impacted directly
abutted the project.  Houses on the opposite side of all these
streets showed no increase in sales activity and no panic listing
at sub-market price.

GARY NETZER                                    May 20, 1983
Page 3


A better example, according to Ms. Backstrom, was to be
found at 10655 Tujunga Canyon Boulevard at the southeast
corner of Tujunga Canyon Boulevard and Hillrose Street.
Here, a 23 unit HUD low and moderate income three story
structure was recently constructed amid, per Ms. Backstrom,
much neighborhood furor. The net effect upon neighborhood
values was almost nil. Only one house that was listed during
or after construction sold below market and that house backed
up to the project and had a pool. Houses once removed and
down the block all sold at market. It was the collective
feeling of the staff at Red Carpet Realty that should the
subject project be built the adjoining properties might suffer
a damage, but that properties a short distance away would most
probably go unaffected and that Mr. Finn's property would most
definitely be unaffected.

Mr. Blair, who claims a 20 year knowledge of Shadow Hills,
reiterated the same feelings, noting that a HUD project now
under construction on Sunland Boulevard, between Nassau and
McVine Avenues, has not even caused an increase in listings
in the immediate neighborhood.

It appears from these discussions that for this area HUD or
other high density projects have little or no effect upon the
value of the immediate neighborhood with the exception of
abutting properties where the loss of privacy is the detri-
mental factor.

The unique factor which separates the subject project from the
rest, however, appears to be the "horse area" question. The
people of Shadow Hills fear the threat to their rural horse-
keeping way of life that a high density multifamily project
would bring.

It is possible, in this case, that the effect of such a project
would be more far reaching than in the aforementioned HUD pro-
jects. However, the appraiser does not feel that this possible
negative aspect would in any way affect Mr. Finn's property for
three reasons. First, as previously stated, Mr. Finn's Lot is
over 700 feet away on the opposite crest of the hill. It would
be totally isolated visually and noisewise from the project.
Second, Mr. Finn's Lot has its orientation to the north. Pro-
spective buyers would be buying his lot for its view of Tujunga
Wash. Third, and perhaps most importantly, Mr. Finn's lot is
a steep hillside down from Hillrose Street to Wentworth Street.
It is unsuited, in my opinion, for horsekeeping and the market
would recognize this and the prospective purchaser would most

GARY NETZER                                May 20, 1983
Page 4


likely be someone who wanted to live in a horse area, without
themselves keeping horses.  Should they desire to keep horses,
they would most likely do as some of their neighbors have done
and build their corrals at the bottom of the hill along Wentworth
Street with a flight of steps up to their homes which face on
Hillrose Street.

In any event, it is the opinion of the appraiser that the pro-
ject proposed for Lot 51 Tract 5776 would probably have some
detrimental effect upon immediately adjoining residential
properties; would possibly make some homes along La Canada
Way less attractive to potential horse property buyers due
to increased traffic and would have no effect at all upon
Mr. Finn's property on Hillrose Street.

Conversely, a downzoning would in effect maintain the existing
value patterns of the area which would be a net value plus for
the adjoining owners and those effected by the increased traffic.
However, the downzoning would in the opinion of the appraiser
have no effect upon the value of Mr. Finn's property.

Finally, it must be noted that this juxtaposition of a HUD pro-
ject in a horse keeping area is very unique.  It is impossible
to determine with any great degree of certainty what effect
the project would have if any, until it happens.  However, with
the limited time available, and the experience of similar projects
in roughly similar areas, it appears that the opinion expressed
herein are well within reason.

RWB:ad


APPROVED:



_____________________________
FRANK R. WALLACE
Chief Real Estate Officer I

23         I, NADINE McNULTY, declare as follows:

24         1.  I am a real estate salesperson, licensed by the State of

25 California having been so licensed and remaining in good standing

26 as such licensee for over eighteen (18) years.

27         2.  I make the following declaration based on my own

28 personal experience as a licensed real estate salesperson and on

                                    -1-

facts known to me personally.

3. I am currently employed by FRED SANDS REALTY, INC.,
where for seven years past I have been the top residential realty
sales producer for FRED SANDS and for several years have
accomplished sales in excess of $20 Million per annum of
residential single and multi-family properties.

4. Prior to my present tenure at FRED SANDS REALTY, INC., I
was employed at GEORGE ELKINS REALTY, INC., where I was for a
period of six years, until my move to FRED SANDS, the top
residential property sales producer having accomplished this
distinction in the sale of single and multi-family residential
properties also.

5. During my licensure as a real estate sales person, I
have sold literally several dozen apartment buildings and several
hundred single family residences in the City and County of Los
Angeles. As a result of my involvement in these sales, I have
special knowledge, skill, experience, training and education in the
valuation and appraisal of residential real property in the City of
Los Angeles. As a further result of this expertise I have special
knowledge, skill, training and education in the marketing and
valuation inter-relationship between single family residences and
any nearby multi-family residential projects.

6. At the request of JAMES B. MOORE and his attorney, WM.
HARRISON NICHOLAS, I have reviewed the proposed low to moderate
income apartment building project sought to be constructed at 9505
La Canada Way, Shadow Hills/Sundland, California by FAIR AVENUE
DEVELOPMENT and, further, have reviewed some thirty properties
owned by Councilman HOWARD A. FINN and/or related individuals and

entities such as RESIDENTIAL RENTAL CO., INC., and FINMORE, INC.

(Attached hereto and fully incorporated herein as Exhibit "A" are

true and correct copies of said thirty property profiles,

consecutively numbered 1 through 30, identifying property

personally owned by HOWARD A. FINN or owned by HOWARD A. FINN

through personal family relationships or his ownership interest in

California corporations).

7. Based on the above-referenced review of Councilman

FINN's interests in real property, and with particular reference to

property numbers 1 and 15 (Property Number 1, located at 9455

Hillrose Street, less than 800 feet from the subject project in

Shadow Hills/Sunland, and Property Number 15, eight adjoining lots

on Wentworth Street, less than 2600 feet from the subject project

in Shadow Hills/Sunland, California), it is my considered belief

and opinion due to my special knowledge, skills, experience,

training and education that the prohibition by Councilman FINN of

the construction of the subject apartment building project at 9505

La Canada Way will have a substantial and direct material financial

effect on the value of Councilman FINN's real property interests

referred to hereinabove, particularly numbers 1 and 15, such that

said prohibition by Councilman FINN will cause the value of said

properties to be enhanced and to appreciate.

8. Based on the above-referenced review of Councilman

FINN's interests in real property, and with particular reference to

property profile numbers 1 and 15, it is my considered opinion and

belief that the direct material financial benefit to be derived by

Councilman FINN as a result of his attempt to eliminate by downzone

the project at 9505 La Canada Way is distinguishable from any

benefit or effect said downzoning and prohibition will have on the

2 general public due to the sheer volume of equity interests owned by

3 Councilman FINN (approximately 26, 100% ownership equity interests

4 in undeveloped lots zoned RA-1) and their approximate market valu-

5 in excess of One Million Dollars ($1,000,000.00). Councilman

6 FINN's own year disclosures on Schedule 721, "Interests In Real

7 Property", pursuant to Government Code Section 820033 and 87206,

8 show a minimum reportable value of $370,000.00. (Attached hereto

9 and fully incorporated herein as Exhibit "B" is a true and correct

10 copy of Councilman FINN'S Statement of Economic Interests, filed

11 March, 1982, pages 1, 2, 3-3(e), 4 and 4(a).)

12          I declare under penalty of perjury that the foregoing is

13 true and correct of my own knowledge except as to those matters

14 which are stated on information and belief, and as to those matters

15 I believe them to be true.

16          Executed this 10th day of May, 1983 at Los Angeles,

17 California.

     Attorneys for Plaintiffs
6

7

8                        SUPERIOR COURT OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA

9                        FOR THE COUNTY OF LOS ANGELES

10

11   FAIR AVENUE DEVELOPMENT CO., a              ) NO.
     California General Partnership              )
12   JAMES B. MOORE, an individual,             ) PLAINTIFF'S MEMORANDUM OF
                                                 ) POINTS AND AUTHORITIES IN
13                                               ) SUPPORT OF APPLICATION
                                                 ) FOR TEMPORARY RESTRAINING
14                 Plaintiffs                    ) ORDER AND ORDER TO SHOW
                                                 ) CAUSE RE PRELIMINARY
15                   vs.                         ) INJUNCTION AND DECLARATIONS
                                                 ) IN SUPPORT THEREOF
16   CITY OF LOS ANGELES, a Chartered City;)
     CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF LOS             )
     ANGELES, a Legislative Body; HOWARD         )
17   A. FINN, an individual and in his          )
     capacity as a City Councilman for the )
18   City of Los Angeles; RESIDENTIAL            )
     RENTAL CO., INC., a California             )
19   Corporation; FINMORE, INC., a              )
     California Corporation; and DOES I          )
20   through XX, inclusive,                      )
                                                 )
21                   Defendants                  )
                                                 )
22   _______________________________________________)

23              Plaintiffs submit the following memorandum of Points and

24   Authorities in order to demonstrate clearly and convincingly to

25   this Court that the Temporary Restraining Order sought by this

26   application is the only form of relief available to plaintiffs to

27   prevent a flagrant abuse of power as a result of the serious

28   conflicts of interest of Los Angeles City Councilman Howard Finn

and the irreparable injury to plaintiffs which would result if

this court does not grant the requested relief.

The following discussion will show that the power to

restrain the acts of elected officials, including Councilman Finn

and the Los Angeles City Council, has been expressly conferred

upon this Court.

This memorandum will then discuss plaintiffs' Declaration

and Verified Complaint in light of the applicable statutory and

case law and will show that this evidence demonstrates a strong

probability that plaintiffs' claims will prove successful at a

trial on their merits. It will show how the type of irreparable

injury with which plaintiffs are threatened, when balanced

against the minor hardship which would be suffered by the City of

Los Angeles indicates that this is indeed an appropriate case for

the granting of temporary prohibitive relief and that plaintiffs

have no adequate alternate remedy of any kind.

Finally, this memorandum will discuss plaintiffs' proposed

Order in order to show that the Order is clear, unequivocal, and

capable of being enforced by this Court.

I.

STATEMENT OF FACTS

This action is brought by plaintiffs FAIR AVENUE

DEVELOPMENT Co. and its principal partner, J. B. MOORE, to enjoin

the enactment of a proposed ordinance and enforcement of another

which openly violate the conflict of interest prohibitions of the

Political Reform Act of 1974, as amended (Gov. Code Sections

81000, et seq, specifically 87100 and 87103). Pursuant to an

1  actual "invitation" from the City of Los Angeles directed to

2  plaintiffs requesting their participation in the City's efforts

3  to generate new low to moderate income apartment housing in Los

4  Angeles pursuant to its 1982 Multi-Family Residential Mortgage

5  Bond Program, plaintiffs have invested well in excess of $125,000

6  in successfully seeking approval of and funding for a low

7  interest construction loan pursuant to said Bond Program on a

8  proposed low to moderate income apartment housing project to be

9  constructed by plaintiffs at 9505 La Canada Way, Shadow

10 Hills/Sunland, California.

11         As is revealed by Councilman FINN's Statement of Economic

12 Interest dated May 31, 1982 (see Exhibit "B" to Declaration of

13 NADINE McNULTY in support hereof), Councilman FINN personally, or

14 through wholly owned business entities, owns some 30 separate

15 equity interests in properties within the Shadow

16 Hills/Sunland/Tujunga area worth a minimum declared value on the

17 date of filing in excess of $371,000.  Many of these properties

18 are undeniably within the scope of material financial effect

19 directly relating to the construction of plaintiffs' proposed

20 City sponsored apartment housing project.  Eight such properties

21 are within 2,500 feet of the subject City sponsored project at

22 9505 La Canada Way, one is less than 750 feet away and, as is

23 shown in the Declaration of NADINE McNULTY in support hereof, at

24 the very least, these nine properties will clearly be enhanced in

25 value should the subject legislation be enacted and enforced.

26 Moreover, Councilman Finn is sole owner of a construction company

27 (Finmore, Inc.) as well as a rental property management company

28 (Residential Rental Company, Inc.) both of which could be

requested relief, the serious harm it would avert, and the

May 5, 1983 
 
Attention: 
Barbara ~1ilman 
Dear Commission Members: 
Complaint of William Harrison Nicholas against 
Councilman Howard Finn 
The letter of complaint dated April 6, 1983 addressed to Ira 
Reiner, City Attorney, is peppered with innuendo and misin-
formation mixed in with a little factual material. 
I will 
respond to that portion which is relevant to the charge that 
I have any conflict of interest. 
1. 
The process of changing a zone to conform to the 
General Plan is mandated by AB 283 (copy enclosed) and is a 
continuous process 
n various parts of Los Angeles. 
The sub-
ject zone change (also known as City Plan Case 30711) is cen-
trally located in the Shadow Hills area where an underlying 
zone of C2 is in conflict with the General Plan-designated 
use of "Very low In, permitting a housing density of one to 
two units per acre. 
In prior years two C2 properties with 
frontages on Sunland Boulevard (a secondary highway) were 
developed--one is a feed and grain outlet, and the other is a 
sanitorium. 
There also exists one three-unit apartment house 
adjoining the feed and grain outlet. 
The only other commer-
cial property in Shadow Hills has limited use and fronts on 
Sunland Boulevard at the corner of Wheatland Avenue--more 
than a mile from the subject property. 
The remainder of 
Shadow Hills has developed as a modest low-density (1/2 acre 
lots) semi-rural horsekeeping residential area. 
Examination of the District Plan reveals the requirement that 
the Commercial use property be h 
oriented. 
The su 
ect 
property is on LaCanada 
,a local street. 
Sunland B u1 -
ard is the 
n 
h 
trav 
s ng Shadow Hills. 
The Shadow Hills Property Owners Association and many local 
residents have requested that the zone be changed to confo m 
to the Di tri t Plan. 
There wer 
sev 
a1 sp ak rs fr 
he 
e 
u Ii 
r 
b 
r 
th 
Cit 
un il a 
he 
un 
u ly 
eq 
t 
t 
ad pti 
t I t  
emove the threat of incompatible land use. 


Fair Political Practices Commission 
May 5, 1983  
The Planning Commission Chief Examiner also recommended the change 
of zone as being "consistent with the public necessity, convenience, 
general welfare, and good zoning practice" (copy of report enclosed) 
and the City Planning Commission unanimously adopted his recommenda-
tion. 
In my opinion, the zone change is necessary to comply with the intent 
of the District Plan (copy enclosed, adopted more than a year before 
I took office) and to meet the requirement of AB 283. 
I believe the 
community will benefit from assurance that any development will be 
compliant with the surrounding neighborhood land uses. 
2. 
On the question of my personal financial interests, the only 
property I have any interest in within the general vicinity is a 
single RA-I-K lot which scales about 700 feet distant on a flat map, 
and about 1300 feet by street from the subject property. 
Whereas the 
subject property is located at the corner of LaCanada Way and LaCanada 
Place facing southwesterly, my property is northerly on Hillrose Street 
(facing in the opposite direction) at the intersection of Radwin Avenue 
and Wayside Drive--both unpaved streets. 
Topographically, the subject 
property spans a small canyon which drains the nearby hills, and my 
property lies on the other side of a ridge--between Hillrose and 
Wentworth Streets (about 150 feet lower). 
My lot overlooks the 
Tujunga Wash and the 210 Freeway. 
I cannot imagine any kind of rela-
tionship the use of the subject lot would have on the value of my prop-
erty when the aspects are so divergent and the spatial relationship so 
remote. 
3. 
On the issue of preventing the construction of the "47-unit 
low- to moderate-income rental apartment", the applicant did not have 
a commitment from the Community Development Department (please-8ee 
attached letter from the City's Community Development Department). 
The alleged commitment involved market rate units with a reservation 
of 20 percent of the units for low- and moderate-income families. 
I 
gather that the inference of the complaint is that such a project would 
lower the property values. 
Without judging the accuracy of the infer-
ence, I hasten to point out that Section 12.02 of the Los Angeles 
Zoning Code requires City decision makers "to conserve and stabilize 
the value of property." 
4. 
I have no operating business activities in the area (all oper-
ations have been suspended since 1980), and I cannot conceive any way 
in which my financial status could be affected by any use of the sub-
ject 
roperty. 


Fair Political Practices Commission 
May 5, 1983  
I hope this letter and the enclosures will provide you with the appro-
priate information to make your findings. 
Due to the cloud cast by 
this allegation, the City Council has continued this item until May 18. 
I would appreciate your prompt response so that the City Council may 
proceed properly. 
Thank you for your interest.
sections.facts:
The facts which have been presented to me are as follows: 
You own a number of parcels of property in Shadow Hills, 
including a parcel locatea at 9455 Hillrose Street. 
James B. 
Moore owns a parcel at 9505 La Canada Way in Shadow Hills. 
Your 
property is approximately 700 feet away from the Moore 
property. 
It is not adjacent to the Moore property, and the 
Moore property is not visible from your property. 
Your property 
is on the otner side of a hill from the Moore property, and 
faces away from that property. 
Your property is undeveloped. 
The Moore prDpertyis located at the intersection of 
La Canada Way and Sunland Boulevard. 
This property is presently 
zoned to permit retail commercial use or multiple residential 
use (C2-l-K). 
Other property in the immediate area on Sunland 
Boulevard is also zoned C2-1-K, and has some commercial use. 
The surrounding property, including yours, is zonea for 
horsekeeping and low density residential use (RA-l-K). 
The 
owner of tne Moore property plans to use it for construction of 
a 47-unit apartment complex. 
At least some of the planned 
apartments woula be for low and moderate income resicents. 
As a member of the City Council, you introduced a motion to 
down zone the Moore property from retail commercial and multiple 
residential use (C2-l-K) to low density residential use 
(RA-l-K). 
The effect of the proposea downzoning would be to 
prevent the construction of the 47-unit apartment complex on the 
Moore property. 


Howard Finn 
May 26, 1983  
At the request of the City Attorney, two Los Angeles City 
Assessors, Josepn A. Gary and Richard W. Brown, assessed the 
potential impact of the downzoning on your properties. 
In their 
reports, both Mr. Gary and Mr. Brown described in detail their 
factual observations and the basis for their opinions. 
We have 
reviewed both reports. 
The first report was made by Joseph Gary. 
He based his 
report on his observations of the properties in question, on 
Planning Department recommendations and on discussions with 
local real estate brokers. 
He stated that the downzoning of the 
Moore property would beneficially affect the contiguous 
properties. 
Properties which are not ao]acent to the Moore 
property, or from which the Moore property is not visible, would 
not be affected by the downzoning. 
Thus, Mr. Gary concluded 
that the downzoning would have no effect on the value of your 
property in Shadow Hills, including your property on Hlllrose 
Street. 
Mr. Gary stated that his evaluation of the effect of the 
construction of the planned apartment complex was speculative 
and conjectural. 
He stated that the proposed project would 
affect the value of surrounding properties, although possibly 
only on a temporary basis. 
The effect could reach as far as 
your Hillrose Street property (but not to your otner Shadow 
Hills property). 
However, he stated that your property, being 
low in value for the area, not suitable for norsekeeping, and 
out'of sight of the project, would be affected less than other 
property in the area. 
Mr. Brown based his report on observation of the properties, 
discussion with four realtors within a two mile area of the 
Moore property, 'and on the experience of the local realtors with 
other housing projects and conaominiums which had recently been 
built in the area. 
These other projects had had very little 
effect on surrounding property values. 
What effect they did 
have was limited to the adjacent properties. 
It was Mr. Brown's 
conclusion that neither the downzoning nor the construction of 
the proposed apartment complex would affect the value of your 
Hillrose Street property or any other property you own in Shadow 
Hills. 
He stated that the effects, if any, of the downzoning or 
of the project would be limited to properties immediately 
adjacent to, and visible from, the project. 
Subsequent to your request to us for advice in this matter, 
litigation has been commenced on the same issue by Mr. Moore, 
through his attorney Mr. Nicholas. 
We nave been furnished with 
copies of some of the pleadings, including a declaration of 


Howard Finn 
May 26, 1983  
Nadine McNulty. 
The question of the effect of the downzoning 
was reviewed by Ms. McNulty, a real estate salesperson in 
Los Angeles, in her declaration submitted to the court. 
She 
stated that she reviewed the proposea apartment project and some 
30 properties owned by you and/or related individuals or 
entities in the ShadOW Hills area. 
Her opinion is that both the 
downzoning and the proposed project would have a material 
financial effect on your properties, particularly on your 
Hillrose Street property (less than 800 feet from the Moore 
property) and your Wentworth Street property (2,600 feet from 
the Moore property). 
She gives no estimate of the amount of the 
effect, nor does sne provide any addit~onal factual information 
about these properties, or about the factors upon Which she 
bases her conclusion. 
We have also considered the materials attached to your 
letter and the complaint1/ of Mr. Nicholas.
sections.analysis:
Government Code Section 871001/ requires a public official 
to disqualify himself from participation in any decision in 
which he has a financial interest. 
An official has a financial 
interest in a aecision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
aecision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable 
from its effect on the public generally, on any real property in 
which the official has an investment of more than $1,000. 
Thus, 
you_will have to disqualify yourself from the decision on the 
proposed downzoning if the following four conditions are met: 
(1) 
you have an interest of over $1,000 in real 
property; 
(2) 
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision 
will have a financial effect on the real property; 
(3) 
the effect will be material; and 
(4) 
the effect will be d~fferent than the effect on 
the public generally. 
!/ Both the complaint and your request are being treated 
by the Commission in an advice context. 
There is no active FPPC 
enforcement investigation of this matter. 
~/ All statutory references are to the Government Code 
unless otherwise noted. 


Howard Finn 
May 26, 1983  
The first condition is clearly met by your ownership of the 
Hillrose Street property. 
The second condition, on the basis of the facts available to 
us, does not appear to be met. 
Both assessors have agreea that 
the downzoning will have no effect on the value of any of your 
property. 
One assessor states that the proposed apartment 
project would have no effect on your property. 
The other says 
that it might have a temporary effect of unknown amount on your 
Hillrose Street property; he also says that such an effect is 
"speculative and conjectural." 
In order for an effect to be 
"reasonably foreseeable," there must be some substantial 
probablity that it will occur. 
Mere conjecture or speculation 
is not sufficient. 
Opinion requested by Tom Thorner, 1 FPPC 
Opinions 198, 203-204 (1975). 
It is true that Nadine McNulty concluded that the downzoning 
and the project would have a material financial effect on your 
properties. 
However, unlike the assessors, she merely stated 
her conclusion; she did not provide any facts upon Which such a 
conclusion could be based. 
Therefore, her declaration has not 
caused us to question the facts providea by you or the facts in 
the assessors' reports. 
Her opinion on the legal issue of 
whether a conflict exists, absent supporting facts, does not 
affect our conclusion. 
In addition to the facts presented by the assessors' 
reports, we also have your letter, a complaint filed by 
Mr. Nicholas, a map of the area and other documents. 
None of 
these items provided any additional information which was 
helpful to us in determining how the proposed downzoning or the 
apartment proje~t would affect your properties. 
Based upon the facts available to us, it does not appear to 
be reasonably foreseeable that either the proposed downzoning, 
or the construction of the proposed apartment complex, will have 
a financial effect upon the value of your properties. 
Therefore, it is not necessary to consider the issues of 
materiality or of the public generally. 
Our advice to you is 
that you do not need to disqualify yourself from the decisions 
on the proposed downzoning as a result of your real property 
investments in Shadow Hills. 
I would like to emphasize that this advice is based on the 
facts that you and tne City Attorney have providea. 
This agency 
is not a fact finding agencYi we do not resolve disputed issues 
of fact. 
Our advice is based on our legal analysis of the facts 
as stated; if the facts turn out to be different than those 


Howard Finn 
May 26,1983  
provided, our advice may change. 
Of course, the court can 
resolve factual disputes. 
If tne court makes a different 
determination from our interpretation, you should not rely on 
our aavice. 
In the meantime, so long as you have requested 
advice in good faith, and have disclosed truthfully all material 
facts, you are protectea by tne provisions of Section 83114(b) 
if you act in reliance on our advice. 
Some question was also raised in Mr. Nicholas' complaint and 
in your letter as to the possible effects of the downzoning 
decision on your business interests. 
You have statea thac you 
have had no business activities in the area since 1980. 
On the 
basis of that statement, and in tne absence of any facts showing 
that the downzoning would have an effect on your business, we 
conclude that you have no potential conflict of interest in the 
downzoning decision as a result of your business interests. 
I hope this letter has answered your questions. 
I will be 
happy to discuss this matter if you have any further questions.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Councilman Howard Finn may vote on a proposed downzoning of property in Shadow Hills that would prevent construction of a 47-unit apartment complex, given his ownership of nearby property in the same area.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that Councilman Finn need not disqualify himself because it is not reasonably foreseeable that the downzoning will have a material financial effect on his properties, based on assessor reports finding no effect on his properties from either the downzoning or the proposed apartment project.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Complete advice letter with clear QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections. Document includes supporting materials (assessor reports, declaration, City Attorney cover letter) that provide detailed factual analysis. FPPC emphasizes advice is based on facts provided and notes that if facts differ, conclusion may change.

embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 307 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-4441 322-5444 May 26, 1983 Howard, Finn Los Angeles City Councilmember City Hall, Room 290 Los Angeles, CA 90012 Re: Advice No. 83-136 Dear Mr. Finn: You have requested advice from the Commission pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) as to whether you may vote on a motion pending before the City Council to downzone certain property in Shadow Hills. FACTS The facts which have been presented to me are as follows: You own a number of parcels of property in Shadow Hills, including a parcel located at 9455 Hillrose Street. James B. Moore owns a parcel at 9505 La Canada Way in Shadow Hills. Your property is approximately 700 feet away from the Moore property. It is not adjacent to the Moore property, and the Moore property is not visible from your property. Your property is on the other side of a hill from the Moore property, and faces away from that property. Your property is undeveloped. The Moore property is located at the intersection of La Canada Way and Sunland Boulevard. This property is presently zoned to permit retail commercial use or multiple residential use (C2-1-K). Other property in the immediate area on Sunland Boulevard is also zoned C2-1-K, and has some commercial use. The surrounding property, including yours, is zoned for horsekeeping and low density residential use (RA-1-K). The owner of the Moore property plans to use it for construction of a 47-unit apartment complex. At least some of the planned apartments would be for low and moderate income residents. As a member of the City Council, you introduced a motion to downzone the Moore property from retail commercial and multiple residential use (C2-1-K) to low density residential use (RA-1-K). The effect of the proposed downzoning would be to prevent the construction of the 47-unit apartment complex on the Moore property. Howard Finn May 26, 1983 Page 2 At the request of the City Attorney, two Los Angeles City Assessors, Joseph A. Gary and Richard W. Brown, assessed the potential impact of the downzoning on your properties. In their reports, both Mr. Gary and Mr. Brown described in detail their factual observations and the basis for their opinions. We have reviewed both reports. The first report was made by Joseph Gary. He based his report on his observations of the properties in question, on Planning Department recommendations and on discussions with local real estate brokers. He stated that the downzoning of the Moore property would beneficially affect the contiguous properties. Properties which are not adjacent to the Moore property, or from which the Moore property is not visible, would not be affected by the downzoning. Thus, Mr. Gary concluded that the downzoning would have no effect on the value of your property in Shadow
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter to Los Angeles Councilman Howard Finn regarding whether he must disqualify himself from voting on a downzoning motion affecting property in Shadow Hills where he owns nearby parcels. The FPPC concludes no disqualification is required because the downzoning will not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on his properties.
